core_intuitions: The four parallelism axes each shard a DIFFERENT thing: TP splits a weight matrix WITHIN a layer, PP splits the stack of layers, EP spreads MoE experts, DP replicates the whole model. TP/PP/EP reduce per-GPU memory; DP only multiplies throughput.

Tensor parallelism works via a column-parallel matmul (input replicated, output partitioned, NO communication) immediately followed by a row-parallel matmul that produces PARTIAL results, which an all-reduce SUMS to recover the true output — two all-reduces per transformer layer.

Communication cost is what decides where a strategy is legal: a TP all-reduce moves only ~2*d_model bytes per rank and is trivial on NVLink but crippling across PCIe/InfiniBand, which is why TP must stay inside an NVLink island.

Expert parallelism turns each MoE layer into a scatter-compute-gather: a router picks experts, an all-to-all DISPATCHES each token to the GPU that owns its expert, experts compute locally, and a second all-to-all GATHERS results back — so throughput depends on batches large enough that every expert GPU gets work.

In inference PP is latency-neutral for decode (stages run strictly sequentially, one token at a time, so no speedup) and is fundamentally a memory-capacity tool, whereas DP is pure throughput at constant latency.

existing.name: mgpu-decision-tree
existing.improvement: Renders cleanly and legibly in both light and dark themes: clear diamond-decision vs outcome-box vs action-box vocabulary, an explicit legend, correct top-to-bottom reading order, and only small illustrative thresholds (no invented benchmark numbers). It correctly serves the 'When Each Strategy Helps' decision-framework section. No change needed.

new_figures.name: four-parallelism-axes
new_figures.concept: One organizing infographic contrasting how TP, PP, EP, and DP each distribute the SAME small model across a grid of GPUs.
new_figures.rationale: This is the mental scaffold for the entire chapter. Readers routinely conflate the axes ('is TP the same as splitting layers?'). A single side-by-side that shows each axis cutting a different dimension of the model makes the taxonomy click before any math, and lets the rest of the chapter hang off it. Nothing in the chapter currently visualizes the axes; the opening table is all text.
new_figures.placement: The Four Parallelism Axes (immediately after the axis table)
new_figures.brief:
A 2x2 panel grid, each panel a small titled card for one axis, using the SAME reference model: a tiny transformer drawn as a vertical stack of 4 layer-blocks (each block = an 'attn' bar + an 'MLP' bar). Show 2 GPUs per panel for clarity (label GPU0 / GPU1), theme-neutral outline boxes with a subtle fill.

Panel TP (Tensor Parallel, 'splits WITHIN a layer'): draw ONE layer block widened; slice it vertically so GPU0 owns the left half of the weight matrix and GPU1 the right half. Under it a small badge: 'all-reduce per layer' with a two-headed arrow between GPU0 and GPU1. Caption: 'each GPU holds a slice of every weight matrix'.

Panel PP (Pipeline Parallel, 'splits the STACK'): the 4-layer stack cut horizontally — layers 1-2 on GPU0, layers 3-4 on GPU1 — with a single one-way arrow (activations) crossing the GPU boundary. Caption: 'each GPU holds a contiguous range of layers'.

Panel EP (Expert Parallel, 'spreads the EXPERTS'): show one MLP block expanded into a row of 4 small expert squares (label a shared router node above); experts 1-2 sit on GPU0, 3-4 on GPU1, with a two-headed 'all-to-all' arrow between them. Caption: 'MoE experts partitioned across GPUs'.

Panel DP (Data Parallel, 'REPLICATES everything'): draw the full 4-layer stack twice, one complete copy on GPU0 and an identical complete copy on GPU1, fed by two separate incoming request arrows from a small 'router' box; NO arrow between the GPUs. Caption: 'full model copied; no cross-GPU comms'.

Add a one-line footer strip reading: 'TP / PP / EP reduce per-GPU memory  •  DP only multiplies throughput'. Keep every label legible, no overlaps; consistent GPU-box styling so the eye compares the CUT, not the decoration. Theme-safe: use neutral strokes and a single accent only for the communication arrows.

new_figures.name: tensor-parallel-allreduce
new_figures.concept: The column-parallel then row-parallel matmul pair, showing why the row-parallel output is partial and must be all-reduced (summed) to recover the true result.
new_figures.rationale: This is the single deepest mechanism in the chapter and the one most students get wrong — they don't see WHY there is exactly one all-reduce and why it is a SUM. A concrete data-flow diagram of X (replicated) -> W split by columns -> Y partitioned -> W' split by rows -> partial Z_i -> all-reduce sum makes the algebra in the text physical. Directly serves the 'Mechanism' subsection and the code for ColParallelLinear/RowParallelLinear.
new_figures.placement: Tensor Parallelism > Mechanism (after the row-parallel all-reduce equation)
new_figures.brief:
A left-to-right data-flow across TWO GPUs (draw as two horizontal lanes, GPU0 top, GPU1 bottom), for a single MLP block (up-project then down-project). Use small illustrative shapes, not real dimensions.

Stage 1 — input: a single input activation matrix X on the far left, drawn ONCE and labelled 'X (replicated on both GPUs)' with a fan-out arrow duplicating it into both lanes.

Stage 2 — column-parallel up-projection: in each lane show the weight as a tall matrix split by COLUMNS — GPU0 gets columns W1 (left half shaded one way), GPU1 gets W2 (right half shaded another). Output of each lane is a partition of Y: label GPU0's output 'Y1 = X·W1' and GPU1's 'Y2 = X·W2'. Add a small tag: 'no communication — output is partitioned'.

Stage 3 — row-parallel down-projection: each lane's Y_i feeds a weight split by ROWS (W'1 on GPU0, W'2 on GPU1). Each lane now produces a FULL-WIDTH but PARTIAL result: label 'Z1 = Y1·W'1' and 'Z2 = Y2·W'2', and annotate 'each is only part of the true sum'.

Stage 4 — all-reduce: draw a prominent box spanning both lanes labelled 'ALL-REDUCE (sum)' with arrows from Z1 and Z2 meeting into it, output a single 'Z = Z1 + Z2' on the far right. Add a small caption under the all-reduce: 'moves ~2·d_model bytes per rank — trivial on NVLink, costly across PCIe/IB'.

Across the top place a slim banner: 'column-parallel (no comm)  →  row-parallel (partial)  →  all-reduce (recover)'. Make the SUM the visual climax. Color the two column/row halves consistently so the reader tracks W1's slice into W'1's slice. Theme-safe strokes; reserve one accent color for the all-reduce box. Everything inside the viewBox, no label overlap.

new_figures.name: expert-parallel-scatter-gather
new_figures.concept: One MoE layer under expert parallelism: router assigns each token its top-k experts, an all-to-all dispatches tokens to the GPU owning each expert, experts compute, a second all-to-all gathers results, then a weighted sum combines them.
new_figures.rationale: EP is a large part of the chapter (wide-EP, DeepSeek case study, the small-batch trap) but has no visual. The scatter-compute-gather round trip is genuinely a multi-step motion that a single static frame under-conveys: tokens must be seen LEAVING their home GPU, landing on an expert GPU, and RETURNING. Animating the two all-to-alls also makes the 'small-batch trap' intuitive — if too few tokens flow, most expert GPUs sit idle. Directly serves 'Expert Parallelism for MoE Models' and the EP small-batch warning.
new_figures.placement: Expert Parallelism for MoE Models > MoE Inference Recap (after the two-all-to-all description)
new_figures.brief:
Show 3 GPUs across the top, each labelled with the experts it owns (GPU0: E0,E1 / GPU1: E2,E3 / GPU2: E4,E5) — six small expert squares total, two per GPU. Below GPU0 sits a small batch of, say, 4 colored token dots, each with a tiny top-k tag showing which 2 experts it wants (use small illustrative integers, e.g. token A -> {E0,E3}, token B -> {E2,E4}, etc.). A 'router' node sits above the tokens.

The static frame (must stand alone) shows all four stages laid out as labelled columns left-to-right: (1) ROUTE — router draws top-k arrows from each token to its target experts; (2) DISPATCH all-to-all — arrows carry each token to the GPU holding its expert (tokens fan out across GPUs); (3) COMPUTE — each expert square lights up processing the tokens it received; (4) GATHER all-to-all + weighted sum — arrows carry expert outputs back to the token's home, combined via a small 'Σ w_i' node into a final output token. Label the two all-to-all arrows explicitly '2 all-to-alls per MoE layer'.

Animation (enhancement): sequence the stages — tokens emit routing arrows, then physically travel (dispatch) to their expert GPUs, expert squares pulse as they compute, outputs travel back (gather) and merge at the Σ node. Loop with a brief pause on the assembled result.

Add a small side callout tied to the animation: 'few tokens -> some expert GPUs idle (the small-batch trap); batch enough that every expert gets work.' Keep token colors distinct and consistent from dispatch to gather so the reader tracks each round trip. Theme-safe: neutral GPU/expert boxes, token colors as the only saturated elements, one accent for the all-to-all arrows. No overlapping arrows at the crossing points — route them with clear bends. Everything within the viewBox.

summary: The chapter is concept-rich but figure-poor: its only visual is a strong end-of-chapter decision tree (keep). The three foundational mechanisms — the taxonomy of parallelism axes, the tensor-parallel column/row + all-reduce mechanism, and the MoE expert-parallel scatter-gather — are currently text-and-equation only. I propose three new figures: two crisp static infographics (four-axis taxonomy; TP all-reduce data flow) and one long-animation for the EP dispatch/compute/gather round trip where motion genuinely carries the intuition (and dramatizes the small-batch trap). All briefs stay theme-safe with only illustrative integers and no invented benchmark numbers.
